# Vanadium Oxide Nanozymes with Multiple Enzyme-Mimic Activities for Tumor Catalytic Therapy


Xiangle Zeng, Hairong Wang, Yating Ma, Xue Xu, Xingxi Lu, Yujie Hu, Jihong Xie, Xiu Wang, Yushuai Wang, Xuliang Guo, * Li Zhao, * and Jianchun Li *

Cite This: ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces2023, 15, 13941-13955


# ACCESS


Metrics & More

Article Recommendations

ABSTRACT: Using tumors containing high concentrations of hydrogen peroxide to design nanozymes is a new and effective strategy, and vanadium-based nanomaterials receive increasing attention. In this paper, four kinds of vanadium oxide nanozymes with different valences of vanadium are synthesized by a simple method to verify the effect of valence on enzyme activity. Vanadium oxide nanozyme-III (Vnps-III) with a low valence of vanadium (V4+ ) exhibits good peroxidase (POD) and oxidase (OXD) activities, which can effectively produce reactive oxygen species (ROS) in the tumor microenvironment for tumor treatment. In addition, Vnps-III can also consume glutathione (GSH) to reduce ROS consumption. Vanadium oxide nanozyme-I (Vnps-I) containing a high valence of vanadium (V5+ ) has catalase

Supporting Information

(CAT) activity, which can catalyze hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) into oxygen (O2), which is beneficial to alleviate the hypoxic environment of solid tumors. Finally, a vanadium oxide nanozyme with both trienzyme simulation activity and GSH consumption ability was screened out by adjusting the ratio of V4+ to V5+ in vanadium oxide nanozymes. In cell and animal experiments, we successfully demonstrate that vanadium oxide nanozymes have excellent antitumor ability and high safety, which may bring great potential for clinical cancer treatment.

KEYWORDS: vanadium oxide, nanozymes, multiple enzyme-mimic, glutathione depletion, mixed valences, tumor therapy


# 1. INTRODUCTION


The oxidoreductase system maintains cellular redox homeostasis by regulating reactive oxygen species (ROS) levels during enzyme-involved tumor cell metabolism. This enzyme system can be divided into two categories: One is superoxide dismutase, which can scavenge ROS. It converts superoxide to hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), which is converted to O2and water, thereby reducing intracellular ROS levels and increasing cell viability. 1 -3The other type of enzymes can produce ROS. For example, peroxidase (POD) can convert H2O2into hydroxyl radical ( · OH). Oxidases (OXD) can convert O2to hydrogen peroxide, which increases intracellular ROS levels. 4 -6In addition, the abnormal increase of ROS level will destroy the redox homeostasis of tumor cells, cause oxidative stress, seriously damage the structure and function of macromolecules in cells, and induce apoptosis or necrosis of tumor cells. 7Therefore, introducing enzymes that increase ROS levels in tumor tissues or cells is an effective strategy for treating cancer.

However, POD and OXD are two kinds of natural enzymes, which are composed of proteins or other biological macromolecules. The clinical application of the POD and OXD is extremely limited because of the influence of the physiological environment, the process of production, the purification, and

the high storage costs. Notably, a class of nanomaterials with the same activities as natural enzymes was developed to replace these unstable natural enzymes. Since Fe3O4with POD activities was discovered in2007, 8hundreds of nanomaterials have been reported in which similar activities to natural enzymes were observed. The researchers named these nanomaterials 'nanozymes'. With the continuous development of the nanozyme field, the concept of nanozyme was constantly updated and expanded. Nowadays, we can call nanozymes 'nanomaterials that catalyze the conversion of enzyme substrates into products and follow enzyme kinetics under physiological conditions'. 9,10Nanozymes were used to describe the function of nanomaterials in biological systems, which essentially combine nanoscale and enzyme-like catalytic properties. 11Nanozymes have received extensive attention due to their large surface area, low cost, high stability, long-term

storage, and adjustable activity. 9,10,12,13In recent years, many metal oxide nanomaterials with POD-like activities have been developed, such as zinc oxide, copper oxide, titanium dioxide, cerium oxide, etc., and these nanozymes have been widely used in tumor therapy. 14 -16Among them, vanadium is one of the40essential micronutrients, which has a regulatory role in biological systems. 17,18Vanadium-based nanomaterials have attracted increasing interest in the field of nanozymes due to their intrinsic catalytic activity for classical peroxidase substrates and long-term anti-biofouling ability. 19,20Furthermore, vanadium is a transition metal element, and vanadium oxides (such as VO2, V2O3, V2O5, V6O13, VOx, etc. 21 -23 ) have exhibited a variety of valence states, adjustable redox activity, and good peroxidase-like activity among the different metal oxide nanozymes. 24Therefore, vanadium oxide nanozymes have shown great application potential in ROS-mediated tumor catalytic therapy. Despite the high prospect, to the best of our knowledge, few vanadium oxide nanozymes have been developed so far. 25The previously developed vanadium oxide nanozymes mainly focus on a single enzyme function in vitro, almost ignoring the complexity of the tumor tissue and tumor microenvironment (TME). The highly complex TME limits the catalytic effect of these nanozymes, making it difficult to achieve the desired therapeutic effect. Therefore, the current development trend of vanadium oxide nanozymes should focus on exploring a multifunctional nanozyme developed for TME specificity to achieve efficient tumor nanocatalytic therapy.

Because of the abnormal growth of tumor tissue, TME has some characteristics that are different from the normal tissue environment, such as low pH, hypoxia, H2O2overproduction, and high intracellular glutathione (GSH) concentration. 26 -28Meanwhile, the high level H2O2provided a substrate for vanadium oxide nanozymes to exert POD activity, which was the advantage of nanozymes in tumor treatment. But the high concentrations of GSH can consume ROS generated by nanozymes, which reduces the effect of nanozymes on nanocatalysis therapy. 29 -31Meanwhile, tumor hypoxia is one of the reasons of tumor proliferation and migration, which reduce the therapeutic effect of many chemotherapeutic drugs and nanozymes. 32On the basis of the complexity of TME, researchers have explored and synthesized vanadium oxide nanozymes with multienzyme simulated activity. However, in slightly acidic TMEs, vanadium oxide nanozymes with GSH consumption, simulated POD activity, and simulated CAT activity are rarely developed, which is a significant problem we face.

Vanadium has a variety of valence states, and the common ones are V4+ and V5+ . 33Notably, a recent report indicated that when VOx entered the cells, it could be degraded into vanadium oxide ions or vanadate by the acidic environment in tumor cells. Among them, vanadate (V5+ ) can be reduced to vanadyl (V4+ ) by biological reducing agents (such as GSH). At the same time, vanadyl (V4+ ) can also be oxidized to vanadate (V5+ ) by intracellular oxidants (such as H2O2). In this process, several ROS are produced. 33In addition, V2O5nanoparticles were proven to be able to catalyze the production of O2from H2O2. This finding suggests that the high-valence vanadium (V5+ ) has oxidation ability, which can consume GSH in tumor tissues and convert H2O2into O2. The low-valence vanadium (V4+ ) can catalyze H2O2to produce ROS and exert antitumor effects. Therefore, we can finally screen out a nanozyme with GSH consumption, simulated POD activity, and simulated CAT activity by continuously adjusting the ratio of V5+ /V4+ in vanadium oxides. Moreover, different catalytic effects have been achieved by mediating the valence of elements in some transition metals, such as Fe2+ /Fe3+ , Cu + /Cu2+ , Ce3+ /Ce4+ , etc. 31,34However, the synthesis of vanadium oxide nanozymes mainly includes solvothermal and chemical vapor deposition

methods. These methods need high temperatures, and the valence of nanozymes depends on the vanadium source. Mao et al. synthesized new vanadium oxide nanomaterials by a redox precipitation method under mild reaction conditions, which provided a new idea for the synthesis of vanadium oxide nanozymes. 35

In this study, we used V2O5 (V5+ ) as the vanadium source and NaBH4as a reducing agent to synthesize vanadium oxide nanozymes. We synthesized four new mixed-valence vanadium oxide nanozymes by continuously adjusting the amount of NaBH4and screened out nanozymes with GSH consumption, simulated POD activity, simulated OXD activity, and simulated CAT activity (Scheme1). After injection in vivo, as a result of the EPR effect, nanozymes accumulate in tumor tissues and release V4+ /V5+ in a slightly acidic environment. V4+ catalyzes H2O2to produce toxic · OH. Vanadium oxide is also used as a CAT-mimic to convert H2O2into O2under weakly acidic TMEs. In addition, V5+ depleted GSH through redox reaction, reduced the consumption of ROS, and enhanced the effect of tumor nanocatalytic therapy mediated by · OH. Therefore, the vanadium oxide nanozymes showed excellent POD-mimic activity, CAT-mimic activity, and GSH consumption ability in the tumor tissue (Scheme1).


# 2. MATERIALS AND METHODS


- 2.1. Materials. Polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP10, MW: 58,000, K2932), vanadium pentoxide (V2O5), sodium citrate dihydrate, 3,3 ′ -5,5 ′ -tetramethylbenzidine (TMB), O -phenylenediamine (OPD), glutathione (GSH), 5,5-dithiobis(2-nitrobenzoic acid) (DTNB), trichloroacetic acid (TCA), and dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) were purchased from Macklin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Sodium borohydride (NaBH4) and anhydrous ethanol (C2H5OH) were supplied by Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) was obtained from Titan Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Acetone was purchased from Tianjin Damao Chemical Reagent Factory (Tianjin, China). Methyl thiazolyl diphenyl-tetrazolium bromide (MTT) was purchased from Biofroxx (Einhausen, Germany). 4 ′ ,6-Diamidino-2-phenylindole (DAPI) was purchased from Servicebio (Wuhan, China). Fluorescein isothiocyanate (FITC) and Cy5were provided by Aladdin (Shanghai, China). The Calcein/PI cell viability/cytotoxicity assay kit, reactive oxygen species assay kit, and mitochondrial membrane potential assay kit (JC-1) were purchased from Beyotime Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). The Annexin V-FITC/PI doublestained apoptosis assay kit was from BestBio Co., Ltd. (Nanjing, China). All chemical reagents in this article were used without further purification.

- 2.2. Animal and Cell Culture. The murine breast cancer cell line4T1was provided by Kunming Cell Bank, Chinese Academy of Sciences, and cultured in a DMEM complete medium containing10% fetal bovine serum and1% penicillin -streptomycin. All cells were cultured in a37 ° C cell incubator maintained at5% CO2and95% relative humidity. After the cells entered the logarithmic growth phase, they could be used in subsequent experiments. The animals used in this experiment were kept in accordance with animal ethical standards. The female BALB/c mice, aged4 -5weeks, were purchased from Henan Sikebeisi Biotechnology Co., LTD.

- 2.3. Synthesis of Vnpsx ( x = I, II, III, IV). 2.3.1. Vnps-I. According to Mao's method, V2O5powder (0.2g, 15mL), sodium citrate dihydrate (0.44g, 10mL), PVP (0.2g, 15mL), and H2O2 (150 μ L, 30wt % in H2O) were successively mixed and stirred violently in the air at room temperature for20min. Then, 10mL of water was quickly added to the mixed system, and the reaction was stirred in the air for4h. After the reaction was stopped, the mixture was centrifuged at15,000rpm for5min, and then the same volume of acetone as the reaction system was added to the supernatant. After

- centrifugation (12,000rpm, 10min), the precipitate was collected and washed with a1:1mixture of water and acetone to obtain the product.

- 2.3.2. Vnps-II. V2O5powder (0.2g, 15mL), sodium citrate dihydrate (0.44g, 10mL), PVP (0.2g, 15mL), and H2O2 (150 μ L, 30wt % in H2O) were sequentially mixed and stirred in air at room temperature for20min. Then, NaBH4 (0.04g, 10mL) was rapidly injected into the mixed system, and the reaction was stirred in air for4h. After the reaction was stopped, the mixture was centrifuged at15,000rpm for5min, and then the same volume of acetone was added as the reaction system to the supernatant. The precipitate was then collected by centrifugation (12,000rpm, 10min) and washed with a1:1mixture of water and acetone to obtain the product.

- 2.3.3. Vnps-III. V2O5powder (0.2g, 15mL), sodium citrate dihydrate (0.44g, 10mL), PVP (0.2g, 15mL), and H2O2 (150 μ L, 30wt % in H2O) were sequentially mixed and stirred in air at room temperature for20min. Then, NaBH4 (0.2g, 10mL) was rapidly injected into the mixed system, and the reaction was stirred in air for4h. After the reaction was stopped, the mixture was centrifuged at15,000rpm for5min, and then the same volume of acetone was added as the reaction system to the supernatant. The precipitate was then collected by centrifugation (12,000rpm, 10min) and washed with a1:1mixture of water and acetone to obtain the product.

- 2.3.4. Vnps-IV. V2O5powder (0.2g, 15mL), sodium citrate dihydrate (0.44g, 10mL), PVP (0.2g,15mL), and H2O2 (150 μ L, 30wt % in H2O) were sequentially mixed and stirred in air at room temperature for20min. Then, NaBH4 (0.2g, 10mL) was rapidly injected into the mixed system, and the reaction was stirred in air for8h. After the reaction was stopped, the mixture was centrifuged at15,000rpm for5min, and then the same volume of acetone was added as the reaction system to the supernatant. The precipitate was then collected by centrifugation (12,000rpm, 10min) and washed with a1:1mixture of water and acetone to obtain the product.

- 2.4. Characterizations. The morphology of Vnpsx ( x = I, II, III, IV) was observed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Hitachi, Regulus-8100). Meanwhile, the elemental composition of Vnpsx and the valence state of vanadium (V) were analyzed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Shimadzu, AXIS). Energydispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) was used to analyze the elemental composition of nanozymes. In addition, the hydrated particle size and zeta potential of the four kinds of nanozymes were measured by a Malvern particle sizer (Nano-ZS290), and the UV -vis absorption spectra of the samples were measured by a UV spectrophotometer (UV-2700).

- 2.5. Peroxidase (POD) Simulation Activity and Kinetic Analysis of Vnpsx . The POD mimetic activity of Vnpsx was detected by a UV -vis spectrophotometer using TMB and OPD as substrates. Briefly, H2O2 (150mM) was mixed with Vnpsx (100 μ g/ mL) at different pH conditions (pH7.4and6.0) of PBS. Subsequently, TMB (5mM) or OPD (15mM) was added to the mixed system. The color change of the system was observed after5min, and the UV absorption spectrum of the color reaction was recorded. Furthermore, we investigated the enzymatic kinetics of these nanozymes. The steady-state kinetics of these vanadium oxide nanozymes was determined by using Vnpsx (100 μ g/mL) as the catalyst in the PBS (pH7.4and6.0) with H2O2and TMB. Meanwhile, different concentrations of H2O2 (Vnps-III and Vnps-IV at pH7.4: 10, 20, 50, 100, and150mM and at pH6.0: 2, 5, 10, 20, and50mM; Vnps-II at pH6.0: 100, 150, 200, 300, and500mM) were used as substrates, and the reaction was detected by measuring the absorbance changes after different reaction times. According to the Michaelis -Menten saturation curve, Lineweaver -Burk curve, and formula1, the maximum catalytic rate Vmax and Michaelis constant Km of enzymatic reaction constants were calculated.

V is the enzymatic reaction rate, Km is the Michaelis constant, [ S ] is the substrate concentration, and Vmax is the maximal reaction velocity.

- 2.6. Oxidase (OXD) Mimetic Activity of Vnpsx . The OXDmimicking activity of Vnpsx was detected by a UV -vis spectrophotometer using TMB and OPD as substrates. Briefly, the four kinds of vanadium oxide nanozymes (100 μ g/mL) were added to PBS (pH7.4and6.0) followed by an equal volume of TMB (5mM) or OPD (15mM). The color change of the system was observed after5min of reaction, and the UV absorption spectrum of the color reaction was recorded.

- 2.7. Catalase (CAT) Mimetic Activity and Kinetic Analysis of Vnpsx . The O2generation was detected by a dissolved oxygen meter to detect the CAT mimetic activity of Vnpsx . Briefly, H2O2 (150mM) was mixed with different Vnpsx nanozymes (100 μ g/mL) in PBS with different pH conditions (pH7.4and6.0). Subsequently, the change of O2content within10min was detected in the reaction system. Furthermore, we investigated the enzymatic kinetics of the four kinds of nanozymes. The steady-state kinetics of these vanadium oxide nanozymes were determined by using Vnpsx (100 μ g/mL) as the catalyst in the PBS (pH7.4and6.0) with H2O2. At the same time, different concentrations of H2O2 (50, 100, 150, 300, and600mM) were used as substrates to detect the reaction by measuring the change in oxygen concentration after different reaction times. According to the Michaelis -Menten saturation curve, Lineweaver -Burk curve, and formula1, the maximum catalytic rate Vmax and Michaelis constant Km of enzymatic reaction constants were calculated.

- 2.8. GSH Depletion with Vnpsx Nanozymes. The ability of Vnpsx to consume GSH was detected by the Ellman method. 36DTNB powder was dissolved in phosphate buffer (pH8.0) with1mM EDTA to prepare the Ellman working solution (4mg/mL). Subsequently, different concentrations of Vnpsx (20, 50, 100, 200, and500 μ g/mL) were mixed with GSH (0.2mg/mL) in the PBS and incubated at37 ° C for4h. Then, 50 μ L of the Ellman working solution was added to the above reaction system and further incubated for20min. Finally, The absorbance at412nm was measured.

To detect the consumption of GSH in tumor cells, 4T1cells were inoculated in96-well plates at2 × 10 3cells/well. After overnight culture, different concentrations of these vanadium oxide nanozymes (1, 2.5, 5, 7.5, and10 μ g/mL) were added for incubation. Subsequently, the cells were incubated with trichloroacetic acid (TCA, 6.5%) at4 ° C for30min. After incubation, 250 μ L of Ellman was added to each well and shaken gently for10min. The OD value was measured at412nm.

- 2.9. In Vitro Cellular Uptake. The4T1cells were seeded in the laser confocal dish at5 × 10 4cells/well density. After overnight culture, the fresh medium with FITC-labeled Vnpsx was added, and free FITC was used as a control. The cells were incubated for1, 2, 4, and8h, respectively. After the incubation, the cells were washed three times with PBS, fixed in4% paraformaldehyde for20min, stained with DAPI for15min, and then washed three times with PBS. The

intracellular fluorescence was observed under an inverted microscope. In addition, we used flow cytometry to investigate cell uptake experiments. 4T1cells were seeded in six-well plates at5 × 10 5cells/ well and incubated according to the above treatment methods. Subsequently, the cells were washed with PBS and collected. After resuspension, the average fluorescence intensity in the cells was detected by flow cytometry.

- 2.10. Detection of Intracellular ROS Production. The ROS level in4T1cells after Vnpsx interference was detected by DCFHDA in the ROS detection kit. In short, 4T1cells were seeded in laser confocal dishes at2 × 10 4cells/well. After overnight culture, the fresh medium containing Vnpsx (2 μ g/mL) was added for24h. Then, the cells were washed with serum-free medium to remove unloaded DCFH-DA. Finally, the cells were washed with PBS and observed under the inverted microscope.

Meanwhile, we used flow cytometry to investigate the production of intracellular ROS. 4T1cells were seeded in six-well plates at5 × 10 5cells/well and treated according to the above method. Subsequently, the cells were washed with PBS and collected. After resuspension, the average fluorescence intensity in the cells was detected by flow cytometry.

- 2.11. In Vitro Cytotoxicity. The toxicity of Vnpsx to4T1cells was investigated by an MTT assay. In short, 4T1cells were inoculated in96-well plates at5 × 10 3cells/well. After overnight culture, the old medium was discarded, and the fresh medium with different concentrations of Vnpsx was added for24h. After incubation, 150 μ L of DMSO was added to each well, and the OD value was detected at490nm after shaking for10min. Then, the survival rate of each group was calculated according to formula2.

OD0is the absorbance at490nm in the blank group, OD1is the absorbance at490nm in the Vnpsx groups, and OD2is the absorbance at490nm in the control group.

- 2.12. Evaluation of Intracellular O2Generation. The4T1cells were seeded in laser confocal dishes at2 × 10 4cells/well. After overnight culture, [Ru(dpp)3] 2+ Cl2with a final concentration of30 μ M was carefully added for4h. Subsequently, the fresh medium containing Vnpsx (2 μ g/mL) was added for30min. Finally, the cells were washed with PBS and observed under the inverted microscope.

- 2.13. Calcein-AM/PI Staining. The4T1cells were seeded in96well plates at a density of1 × 10 4cells/well for overnight culture and then added to the medium with four kinds of vanadium oxide nanozymes for24h. After incubation, according to the requirements of the Calcein/PI cell activity/cytotoxicity assay kit, the cells were stained at37 ° C for30min. Subsequently, the cells were observed under the fluorescence microscope.

- 2.14. Mitochondrial Membrane Potential Changes. The effect of vanadium oxide nanozymes on the mitochondrial membrane potential of4T1cells was investigated by the mitochondrial membrane potential assay kit (JC-1). Briefly, 4T1cells were seeded in the laser confocal dish at2 × 10 4cells/well. After overnight culture, a fresh medium containing Vnpsx (2 μ g/mL) was added and incubated for24h. Subsequently, according to the requirements of the kit, JC-1staining was used for30min. After staining, the cells were washed and observed under the microscope.

- 2.15. Apoptosis. The Annexin V-FITC/PI double staining apoptosis assay kit was used to quantitatively determine the number of apoptotic cells. 4T1cells were seeded in six-well plates at5 × 10 5cells/well overnight. Subsequently, the fresh medium with Vnpsx (5 μ g/mL) was added for24h. All cells were collected, washed, and resuspended in the Annexin V binding solution. The cells were stained with Annexin V-FITC and PI. Then, they were immediately detected by flow cytometry.

- 2.16. Hemolysis Test. Because Vnpsx was administered intravenously, the effect of nanozymes on red blood cells was investigated. In short, normal mouse red blood cell suspension was collected and diluted with PBS to a final concentration of2% (v/v). Subsequently, the diluted red blood cells were mixed with different concentrations of Vnpsx (10, 20, 50, 100, 200, and500 μ g/mL). After incubation at37 ° C for2h, 100 μ L of the supernatant was centrifuged (5000rpm, 5min) to determine the absorbance at570nm. The relative hemolysis rate (RHR%) was calculated by formula3. The PBS solution was the negative control, and the deionized water was the positive control.

A0is the absorbance of the PBS treatment group at570nm, A1is the absorbance of the sample group at570nm, and A2is the absorbance of the deionized water treatment group at570nm.

- 2.17. Biodistribution In Vivo. To perform in vivo experiments, we established the BALB/c mice tumor model. We transplanted4T1cells (1 × 10 6cells per mouse) subcutaneously into BALB/c mice (female, 4 -5weeks old). When the tumor volumes were500mm3 , free Cy5and Cy5-labeled Vnps-III (Cy5dose of10 μ g/mouse) were injected into the mice through the tail vein. The organs (heart, liver,

Figure1. Characterization of the Vnpsx nanozymes. TEM images of (A) Vnps-I, (B) Vnps-II, (C) Vnps-III, and (D) Vnps-IV. EDX spectrum of (E) Vnps-I, (F) Vnps-II, (G) Vnps-III, and (H) Vnps-IV. (I -L) XPS high-resolution survey of V2p in the Vnpsx , respectively.

spleen, lung, and kidney) and tumors of the mice were removed at2, 12, and24h. Then, fluorescence imaging was performed.

2.18. In Vivo Antitumor Effect. When the tumor volume of mice reached100mm3 , all mice were randomly divided into five groups (saline, Vnps-I, Vnps-II, Vnps-III, and Vnps-IV; n = 5per group). The first administration was recorded as day0, and the drugs were administered every3days at a10mg/kg concentration. The length and width of the tumors were recorded with a Vernier caliper before each administration, and the tumor volume was calculated according to formula4.

V is the tumor volume, a is the tumor length, and b is the tumor width.

After five times of administrations, the subcutaneous tumors were removed and photographed. Then, tumor tissues were stained with HE, DCFH-DA, and HIFα immunofluorescence.

- 2.19. In Vivo Pharmacokinetics. To study the pharmacokinetics of Vnps-III, it (10mg/kg) was injected into tumor-bearing BALB/c mice by tail vein injection, and fresh blood was taken from the orbital venous plexus of mice within the predetermined time (0, 0.25, 0.5, 1, 2, 4, 8, 12, and24h). In addition, the organs (heart, liver, spleen, lung, and kidney) and tumors of mice were removed at24h. Finally, all blood samples and tissues were digested with aqua regia, and the vanadium content in blood samples and tissues was determined by ICP-MS.

- 2.20. In Vivo Safety. After treatment with four kinds of vanadium oxide nanozymes, the main organs (heart, liver, spleen, lung, and kidney) of mice were taken for HE staining to evaluate their toxicity to organs. Moreover, the blood was collected to detect aspartate aminotransferase (AST), alanine aminotransferase (ALT), blood urea nitrogen (BUN), and creatinine (CRE) to reflect the in vivo safety of nanoparticles.

2.21. Statistical Analysis. Data are presented as the mean ± standard deviation (SD). Statistical significance ( * p < 0.05, ** p < 0.01) was performed by one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) and nonparametric tests using SPSS.


# 3. RESULTS AND DISCUSSION


- 3.1. Synthesis and Characterization of Vnpsx . According to the method of Mao (Scheme1), we used a new and milder redox method to synthesize vanadium oxide nanozymes. V2O5and PVP were used as vanadium sources and surfactants. Sodium citrate and NaBH4were applied to etch and reduce the agent, respectively. Subsequently, four different mixed-valence vanadium oxide nanozymes were obtained by constantly adjusting the amount of NaBH4. 35The SEM and TEM images indicated that Vnps-I, Vnps-II, and Vnps-III are small round particles with a particle size of about10nm. VnpsIV had an irregular nanosheet structure with a particle size of about55nm (Figure1A -D and Figure S1, Supporting Information). In addition, when detecting the hydrated particle size of Vnpsx by the Malvern particle size analyzer, we found that the size of four kinds of nanozymes increased slightly, which were13 (Vnps-I), 13 (Vnps-II), 15 (Vnps-III), and73nm (Vnps-IV) (Figure S2). Among them, the larger particle size of Vnps-IV may be due to the increase in the aging time of nanoparticles (Vnps-I, Vnps-II, and Vnps-III aging time is4h, but Vnps-IV aging time is8h). The zeta potential results (Figure S3) showed that the surface charges of the four kinds of nanozymes are negatively charged, consistent with previous reports. We observed that the zeta potential of Vnps-IV was bigger than that of Vnps-III because the larger particle size of Vnps-IV made its specific surface area smaller and showed a bigger charge. 37Figure1E -H is the elemental analysis of Vnpsx by energy dispersive X-ray spectrometer (EDX). It showed that Vnpsx contains four kinds of elements: C, O, V, and Na, which proved the successful synthesis of four kinds of vanadium oxide nanoparticles. The excess C element mainly came from the organic matter (PVP, sodium citrate) in the reactants, whereas the Na element was introduced by sodium citrate and NaBH4. Meanwhile, Figure S4in the Supporting Information shows the XPS photoelectron spectroscopy results

In addition, we used XPS to detect the single vanadium element in Vnpsx to analyze the valence composition of vanadium in nanoparticles. As shown in Figure1I -L, with the increase of NaBH4content, V5+ in V2O5was continuously reduced to V4+ . In the synthesized nanoparticles, the vanadium in Vnps-I was pure V5+ , and Vnps-II was mixed V5+ /V4+ . However, the vanadium in Vnps-III and Vnps-IV was V4+ due to the addition of excessive NaBH4. Subsequently, we analyzed the XRD of Vnpsx . As shown in Figure S5, the Vnpsx was a mixed crystal phase of VO2and V2O5matching the standard cards JCDPS31-1438and52-0794. Among them, Vnps-I and Vnps-II had a higher matching degree with V2O5, and Vnps-III and Vnps-IV had a higher matching degree with VO2, which was related to their different V4+ /V5+ ratios. Finally, we characterized these nanozymes by UV -vis spectroscopy. As shown in Figure S6, Vnps-I showed a maximum absorption peak at450nm, and Vnps-II had a broad absorption peak at500 -800nm. Vnps-III and Vnps-IV showed broad absorption peaks at600 -800nm, which were consistent with the previous reports, suggesting that the materials had a potential for photothermal properties. 38

3.2. In Vitro Enzymatic Activity of Vnpsx . Herein, the peroxidase (POD) activity of vanadium oxide nanozymes was investigated by colorimetric analysis. As shown in Figure2A, when nanozymes exert POD activity, the generated ROS can oxidize3,3 ′ ,5,5 ′ -tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) to blue oxTMB and show maximum absorption at about650nm. Furthermore, we used o -phenylenediamine (OPD) as the POD activity detection reagent. Under the action of ROS, OPD was oxidized to orange oxOPD with maximum absorption at450nm. Figure2B shows that Vnps-III and Vnps-IV had better POD simulation activity with the low valence of vanadium in Vnpsx . It is worth noting that the reaction system changed from normal to weakly acidic, and the POD-mimic activity of these nanozymes was increased, suggesting that the nanozymes have a stronger ability to generate ROS in the slightly acidic TME after entering the tumor tissue. In short, regardless of pH7.4or6.0, the POD simulated the activity of the four kinds of vanadium oxide nanozymes: Vnps-III > Vnps-IV > Vnps-II > Vnps-I. It indicated that the proportion of V4+ in the nanoparticles was the key to determining the POD enzyme activity of vanadium oxide nanozymes. In addition, the POD enzyme mimic activity of Vnps-III was higher than that of Vnps-IV, possibly as a result of the smaller particle size and larger catalytic area. The results of OPD detection were consistent with the above experimental results (Figure S7). However, we found an interesting phenomenon, as shown in Figure2C and Figure S8. When the system did not contain H2O2, Vnpsx can still discolor TMB and OPD under acidic conditions, indicating that the synthesized Vnpsx had oxidase (OXD) activity and directly catalyzed oxygen into ROS, 30and the ability of vanadium oxide nanozymes to simulate oxidase activity was of the following order: Vnps-III > Vnps-IV > Vnps-

According to previous reports, vanadium pentoxide showed good CAT simulation activity. 39We tested the ability of Vnpsx to mimic CAT by detecting the increase in oxygen. As shown in Figure2D,E, Vnpsx had the ability to catalyze hydrogen peroxide to produce oxygen, and Vnps-I had the strongest catalytic ability, indicating that the vanadium element that exerted CAT enzyme activity was mainly V5+ . By comparing the simulated CAT activity under different pH conditions, it was not difficult to find that acidic conditions inhibit the corresponding activity because nanozymes mainly exert POD activity under acidic conditions, which was similar to the transition metal elements such as Fe and Mn reported previously. Moreover, we used the Ellman reagent to detect the ability of Vnpsx to consume GSH. In the expected scenario, because of V5+ high valence, it had certain oxidizability for GSH. However, in the actual detection, the ability of Vnps-I and Vnps-II with more V5+ to consume GSH was not as strong as Vnps-III and Vnps-IV (Figure2F and Figure S9). To further investigate the simulated catalytic activity of Vnpsx nanozymes, the steady-state catalytic kinetics of POD

was determined by using H2O2as substrate and TMB as detection substance. The steady-state catalytic kinetics of CAT was determined by the change of oxygen content in the solution. At the same time, under different H2O2concentrations, the absorbance change of the reaction system at650nm and the oxygen content change of the solution were measured to calculate the POD and CAT enzyme catalytic rate. The Michaelis -Menten fitting curve of enzyme catalysis was made with the reaction rate as the ordinate and H2O2as the abscissa. Then, the Lineweaver -Burk fitting curve was drawn by the double reciprocal method to calculate the steadystate constant Vmax (the maximum catalytic rate of the enzymatic reaction; the more significant the Vmax value is, the better is the catalytic effect of the enzyme) and Km (the substrate concentration when the enzymatic reaction rate is half of the maximum rate; the greater the Km value is, the worse is the catalytic effect of the enzyme). 40Figure3A,B shows the enzymatic reaction diagrams of Vnps-III at pH7.4and6.0, respectively. It can be seen from the figure that the Km value of Vnps-III at pH7.4was36.32mM, which was higher than that at pH6.0 (3.90mM). The Vmax value also showed similar results. 3.3. The Cellular Uptake of Vnpsx . Vnpsx exhibited excellent multienzyme catalytic activity, and we next evaluated the therapeutic efficacy of four kinds of vanadium oxide nanozymes at the cellular level. Initially, we investigated Vnpsx uptake by4T1cells before the detailed in vitro cell experiments. FITC-labeled Vnpsx was incubated with4T1cells, and the intracellular fluorescence changes were observed at different time points (1, 2, 4, and8h) to reflect the uptake of different nanozymes by cells. As shown in Figure4A, free FITC was a small molecule that can be rapidly ingested and metabolized by cells. 41Therefore, the intracellular fluorescence intensity quickly decreased after2h. Compared with the FreeFITC group, the groups of these vanadium oxide nanozymes had better cell uptake because the PVP in the nanozymes had better biocompatibility. However, the uptake of Vnpsx by4T1cells reached the maximum at4h followed by a rapid decrease in intracellular fluorescence brightness at8h. We speculated that it was caused by the small particle size of nanozymes. The presence of surfactants can only achieve accumulation in a special period. Furthermore, the results obtained by flow cytometry were consistent with fluorescence microscopy (Figure4A and Figure S15). These results confirm that Vnpsx nanozymes can be effectively absorbed by4T1cells, which was conducive to the catalytic killing of tumor cells.

3.4. In Vitro Therapeutic Effect and Cell Death Mechanism of Vnpsx . Next, the MTT assay was used to detect the toxic effects of four kinds of nanozymes on4T1cells. The results showed that all nanozymes had a good effect on eliminating tumor cells. In addition, statistical analysis between the groups at different concentrations revealed that Vnps-III exhibited better toxicity than the other three groups (Figure4D). The cytotoxicity of these nanozymes to4T1cells was as follows: Vnps-III > Vnps-IV > Vnps-II > Vnps-I. It was consistent with the results of POD and OXD activities simulated by Vnpsx in vitro, indicating that Vnps-III can produce more toxic ROS to induce4T1cell apoptosis. Next, we studied the death mechanism of4T1cells. First, we investigated the production of ROS by vanadium oxide nanozymes in4T1cells using DCFH-DA as an indicator of ROS, which can produce green fluorescence when oxidized by ROS. As shown in Figure4B, after incubating these nanozymes with4T1cells for24h, a large amount of green fluorescence was produced in Vnps-III and Vnps-IV groups, and the results showed that these two nanozymes could produce abundant ROS in the cells. In contrast, the intracellular ROS levels were lower in Vnps-II and Vnps-I groups. Moreover, flow cytometry (Figure4C and Figure S16) showed similar results. After statistical analysis, it was found that the ability of four kinds of nanozymes to induce ROS production was as follows: Vnps-III ≈ Vnps-IV > Vnps-II > Vnps-I. It was also consistent with the

3.5. In Vivo Pharmacokinetic and Distribution of Vnpsx . Considering that the following experiment was administered by tail vein injection, we used the hemolysis test to detect the effect of Vnpsx on blood cells. As shown in Figure S19, the relative hemolysis rates of the four kinds of vanadium oxide nanozymes at100 μ g/mL (consistent with the concentration of in vivo administration) were less than5%, which proved that Vnpsx had little effect on erythrocytes. It was crucial to investigate the pharmacokinetics and distribution of nanomedicines for their biomedical applications. It can also be seen intuitively that the accumulation of nanomedicines on tumor tissues reflected their targeting ability. First, we evaluated the blood circulation curve after intravenous injection of Vnps-III at different time points. As shown in Figure6A and Table3, the circulating half-life of Vnps-III in blood was about4.7h. The pharmacokinetic curve showed a sharp decrease of drug concentration in the first4h. We believed that it is due to the small particle size of Vnps-III and its rapid distribution in mice. In addition, the main organs and tumors of mice were removed after24h of administration, and the relative content of V element in each tissue ( μ g/g) was detected by ICP-MS. As shown in Figure6B, Vnps-III was mainly metabolized through the liver and kidney. Hence, the content of V element was the highest in the liver and kidney. However, the vanadium content in the tumor site reached7.8% due to the EPR effect that Vnps-III accumulated in the

3.6. In Vivo Anticancer Effects of Vnpsx . On the basis of the good antitumor effect in vitro and pharmacokinetic results, we further investigated the antitumor effect of the four kinds of nanozymes in vivo. BALB/c mice bearing4T1cells were randomly divided into five groups ( n = 5): (1) control (saline), (2) Vnps-I, (3) Vnps-II, (4) Vnps-III, and (5) VnpsIV. The nanozymes were given at0, 3, 6, 9, and12days (the control group was given saline), and the dose was10mg/kg. The changes of tumor volume and body weight were recorded every3days, and the experiment ended on day21 (Figure6C). The results were consistent with our expectations (Figure6D,E). Because saline had no therapeutic effect, the tumor volume continued to increase, reaching about19times the initial volume after treatment. However, POD and OXD of Vnps-I showed the worst simulation activities among the four groups of vanadium oxide nanozymes, and CAT simulation activity alone could not achieve an effective therapeutic effect. In contrast, Vnps-III with better-simulated POD and OXD activity had the best effect on tumor treatment and the smallest tumor volume growth at the end of treatment (Figure S21). Next, we measured the tumor weight in each group after treatment, and the results were similar to the tumor volume (Figure6D,E,G). Meanwhile, the tumor growth inhibition rate (TGI) of these nanozymes was calculated according to the tumor volume. It can be concluded from Figure6F that the TGI of the Vnps-III group reached76.1%. It was greater than the other three groups (Vnps-I: 33.5%, Vnps-II: 44.4%, and Vnps-IV: 55.6%). In addition, the body weight changes in mice (Figure6H) showed no significant fluctuation, indicating that these nanozymes had little effect on body weight and good biocompatibility. Subsequently, to further evaluate the therapeutic effect of nanozymes, the isolated tumor tissues were analyzed by HE staining. As seen in Figure7A, the nucleus (blue) of the Vnps-III treatment group was lost in a large area, indicating that the tumor tissues had a large area of apoptosis and the therapeutic effect was better. Moreover, the immunofluorescence results of ROS (Figure7B) showed that a mass of green fluorescence appeared in the Vnps-III group,

The expression of HIFα in the Vnps-I group was the lowest as a result of its CAT activity and the O2production at the tumor site (Figures7C and2D,E), suggesting that Vnps-I had the hidden ability to ameliorate hypoxia in the tumor site. In addition, we examined the expression of GSH in each group (Figures7D). The expression of GSH was the lowest in group Vnps-III, and it was also decreased in the other groups, suggesting that Vnps-III can effectively reduce the GSH in the tumor site, increase the ROS, and more effectively treat the tumor. In summary, Vnps-III showed strong antitumor ability in this part of the experiment, which was related to the low valence of vanadium. Finally, some biochemical indicators in the blood were detected, and HE staining of the main organs was performed to evaluate the potential toxicity of nanozymes. Aspartate aminotransferase (AST) and alanine aminotransferase (ALT) were used to evaluate hepatic function (Figure S22A,B); blood urea nitrogen (BUN) and creatinine (CRE) were used to evaluate renal function (Figure S22C,D). These indicators in the four nanozyme treatment groups showed almost no change compared with the control group, indicating that nanozymes had no effect on hepatic and renal function during treatment. In addition, the HE staining results of organs (Figure S23) were consistent with the above, and there was no obvious pathological abnormality, suggesting that vanadium oxide nanozymes had good histocompatibility and biocompatibility.


# 4. CONCLUSIONS


In summary, we successfully synthesized four kinds of vanadium oxide nanozymes with different valences of vanadium by the redox method. Because of the presence of PVP, Vnpsx has good water dispersibility and biocompatibility. In vitro characterization experiments showed that in Vnps-III and Vnps-IV nanozymes containing V4+ , which could effectively exert POD and OXD activities, the enzyme activity increased under acidic conditions. The Vnps-I with V5+ has good CAT activity and catalyzed H2O2to produce O2. It suggests that the valence of vanadium decreased in nanozymes and the simulated CAT activities decreased, whereas the POD

activities increased. The cell experiments showed that Vnps-III can inhibit4T1cells, which effectively exert POD and OXD enzyme activities. The mechanism of cell death was that a large amount of ROS was produced in the cells, resulting in intracellular redox imbalance and inducing cell mitochondrial membrane potential collapse and apoptosis. Furthermore, Vnps-III could also effectively consume GSH, reduce the consumption of ROS, and increase the intracellular ROS concentration. The results showed that Vnps-III had good antitumor ability, no adverse effects on normal tissues, and high safety in vivo. However, because of the small particle size and the short time of Vnps-III in the blood circulation, the EPR effect was not obvious, and the treatment effect was poor. Therefore, increasing the circulation time and targeting ability in vivo was an urgent problem to be solved. We believe that we can solve these problems by surface modification. The main strategies are as follows: First, the groups with targeting ability (such as folic acid) can be introduced on the surface to increase the uptake of tumor cells. 43Second, polymers, small organic molecules, and inorganic materials can be introduced on the surface, which can influence the catalytic activity of the nanozyme either directly (e.g., PEG and dextroside) or indirectly (protein corona). In addition, these materials can also influence the therapeutic efficacy by influencing their distribution in vivo (cancer cell membrane camouflage) and the intracellular fate (PEI-modified). 44Finally, grafting the structural properties of natural enzymes into the synthesis of nanozymes was an effective strategy to increase their catalytic effect. 45In conclusion, the vanadium oxide nanozymes with the different valences of vanadium provide a basis for the future development of vanadium-based nanozymes.
